If we both dope, we both gain some performance and each protect ourselves from falling behind, but we share serious health risks and a real chance of being caught and banned, so that is clearly worse for each of us than both staying clean, though still better for me than being the only clean one against a doped rival.

Right now I have to decide whether to stay clean or start the doping program, knowing that Jordan is separately deciding whether to stay clean or dope as well.

I am Dana, an operations manager at the same digital health firm now facing a federal fraud case. Alex, a senior product manager, and I both signed off on the billing logic that regulators say systematically overcharged insurers. Investigators separated us and put the same two offers on the table: either stay silent and stick to the company’s story, or testify fully about the scheme and about the other person’s role, with no way for us to coordinate.

If Alex and I both stay silent, the evidence on intent stays thin, so we likely each lose our careers and pay fines but avoid long prison terms; for both of us this shared hit is harsh but still better than any outcome where one of us goes away for a long time. If I stay silent while Alex testifies, prosecutors will treat Alex as the cooperative witness and me as the main target, pushing for many years in prison and financial ruin for my family, while Alex gets the lighter deal; that is clearly my worst case. If I testify while Alex stays silent, I become the key witness and probably avoid prison beyond maybe a token sentence and fines, while Alex takes the severe sentence instead; that is my best personal outcome, even though it is terrible for Alex. If we both testify, we each get several years in prison, which is worse for both of us than if we had both stayed silent but still safer for each of us than being the only one who stayed quiet. So, thinking only about my own fate, testifying leaves me better off than staying silent no matter what Alex decides, even though we would both prefer the more lenient shared outcome where we both stayed silent.

Right now, not knowing what Alex will do, I have to choose between staying silent and holding the line, or testifying fully, while Alex independently chooses whether to stay silent or testify.

I lead content policy for Sphere, the largest social platform in my country. A new emergency decree lets the government order us to remove “destabilizing” political opinions across the internet within hours or face ruinous fines, throttling, or even charges against local staff. Our main rival, Loop, is under the same pressure, and ministers are hinting that the “responsible” platform will get tax breaks, state advertising, and preferential treatment. Users and NGOs are already warning that open debate online is about to vanish.

If both Sphere and Loop refuse broad censorship and only remove clearly illegal content, we share the legal risk but preserve trust, user growth, and our brands; for both of us that outcome is clearly better than turning into heavily filtered channels. If I resist while Loop quietly complies, regulators can make an example of Sphere alone: we could be blocked nationwide, lose advertisers, and watch users and talent flow to Loop, which for me is the worst case and for them the most profitable. If I comply while Loop resists, I become the safe partner for government and big advertisers, gaining revenue and protection while they eat the legal risk and reputational attacks, which is my best short‑term outcome and their nightmare. If we both comply, we each avoid immediate punishment and get official favor, but we jointly destroy trust in public discourse online, and over time we both lose users and influence—still better for each of us than being the lone holdout, yet worse than if we had both resisted.

Today I have to give a final answer to the minister: either Resist by publicly limiting removals to narrow legal standards, or Comply by quietly enforcing their broader blacklist of opinions. At the same time, Loop’s policy chief is making the same choice: Resist or Comply with the same demands.

I am the VP of product at JobSphere, a major recruitment platform finalizing how our new AI hiring engine will be used: it can either provide recommendations that recruiters must approve, or it can autonomously decide shortlists and job offers. Our biggest rival, TalentMatch, is deciding the same thing right now, and clients, regulators, and advocacy groups are all pressing us in different directions about efficiency versus human autonomy and control.

If both JobSphere and TalentMatch Enforce Review, every AI recommendation still needs human approval, so candidates retain meaningful recourse, recruiters keep their judgment sharp, regulators see a responsible balance, and both companies enjoy stable, moderate profits, which is a reasonably attractive outcome for us. If I Enforce Review but TalentMatch Pushes Automation, clients chasing lower costs and speed will move toward them; we lose business and influence while they surge ahead and define how much say humans still have in hiring, which is the worst result for us and the best for them. If we Push Automation while TalentMatch Enforces Review, we capture those clients instead, appear more innovative, and my team looks like a big success, giving us the strongest payoff even though people’s fates lean more heavily on opaque AI decisions. If both of us Push Automation, widespread reliance on our systems weakens human skills, visible unfair outcomes trigger public backlash, and regulators clamp down hard, leaving both firms with damaged reputations and constrained products that are clearly worse than if we had both enforced human review, but still not as bad for us as holding back alone while TalentMatch pushes ahead.

In my briefing to our CEO I must choose whether to recommend that we Enforce Review or Push Automation, while TalentMatch’s executives independently choose whether they will Enforce Review or Push Automation for their launch.

I lead editorial strategy at a competing digital news outlet that fights for the same readers, ad budgets, and search rankings as our main rival. Our board is pressing for growth, and we see daily data showing that emotional, loosely sourced stories drive more clicks than dry, carefully documented reporting. At the same time, platforms, regulators, and major brands are warning that they’ll start cutting off or punishing outlets that repeatedly amplify false or misleading claims.

If I commit to careful verification and they do the same, both our sites accept lower immediate traffic but keep a reputation for reliability, retain premium advertisers, and avoid being restricted or fined, which is a reasonably strong and mutually attractive position. If I verify while they run with every dramatic rumor, my outlet looks slow and boring, I lose audience share and key ad contracts to them, and they pick up big gains in reach and income, making that clearly the worst situation for me and their best. If I loosen standards and push viral, minimally checked content while they stay strict, I get the windfall—fast growth, more influence, and extra revenue—while they are left behind, which is the most attractive situation for me and their least. If we both start exploiting unvetted stories, we may enjoy a brief surge in clicks, but the overall information environment becomes polluted, people stop trusting any outlet, enforcement actions and platform penalties hit us, and advertisers scale back, so both of us end up in a worse place than if we had both verified, yet still better for me than if I had been the only cautious one.

I now have to decide whether to verify rigorously or to exploit viral, thinly checked stories, at the same time that their editor chooses whether to verify or to exploit in the same way.

I lead robustness evaluation at a major AI lab that is about to release a powerful language model for high-stakes uses like medical triage support, legal drafting, and automated financial advice. Our closest competitor is aiming to launch a similarly capable model at the same time, and regulators have warned that serious failures under adversarial or out-of-distribution prompts could trigger heavy fines, forced audits, or emergency shutdowns across the industry.

If we both commit to exhaustive adversarial and robustness testing before launch, we’ll likely catch most dangerous behaviors, satisfy regulators, and each release slightly later with decent profit and strong reputations; that outcome is good for me and, I believe, reasonably good for them too. If I invest fully in exhaustive tests while they quietly limit themselves to a minimal checklist, they can launch first, grab market share, and leave me spending more time and money fixing edge cases, making that scenario clearly the worst for me even though it improves overall safety. If I cut corners and do only a skim-level test while they run exhaustive evaluations, I save huge costs, launch faster, and still benefit from their safer deployment lowering the chance of a sector-wide scandal—this outcome is personally my best case. If we both cut corners and only skim-test, we both ship quickly and cheaply, but a serious robustness failure by either of us could spark public backlash and aggressive regulation that hurts us both more than if we had both slowed down to test thoroughly; that is bad for me, but not as bad as being the only lab that tested thoroughly while the other raced ahead.

So my decision is whether to push for full-scale, expensive stress-testing of our model or to accept a minimal skim test and release early, knowing that our competitor is independently choosing between the same two options.

I lead red-teaming and security for a competing AI company that ships a large language model into sensitive sectors alongside a rival lab whose system we regularly test. Our executives know both models can be abused via prompt injection, covert data scraping, and model-stealing APIs, and that any serious incident could cost us government contracts, investor confidence, and bring aggressive regulators down on us. On a joint call, their security head is suggesting a bilateral commitment that would reshape how both sides run offensive and defensive operations.

If I agree that we will only strengthen our own defenses and they do the same, both companies see fewer breaches, keep training data and model internals safer, and earn a reputation with regulators and clients as trustworthy providers; for us and for them this is clearly a good, stable outcome. If I stick to defense-only while they secretly keep attacking our model, they might extract our model weights, discover and exploit failure modes, and quietly brief key customers and regulators on our weaknesses, giving them a major competitive and compliance advantage while we take the hit; that is my worst scenario and their best. If I maintain our offensive operations against their systems while they sincerely limit themselves to defense, we could gain access to their proprietary capabilities and highlight their weaknesses while our own model remains relatively protected, giving us our best-case advantage and leaving them in their worst position. If both of us keep attacking each other's models, we each leak some IP, spend heavily on constant countermeasures, and risk joint scrutiny that could restrict powerful model deployments for everyone; this is clearly worse for both of us than mutual restraint, but still not as bad for me as being the only side that plays fair while the other side exploits us.

I now have to decide whether to commit us publicly to defense-only toward their model or to keep running active attacks, while they simultaneously choose whether to match that restraint or also keep attacking ours.

I am the top 400m runner on our national team, and in three weeks the trials will decide Olympic spots and major sponsorships. My closest rival is my own training partner; we are expected to dominate, but only one of us is likely to win the big contract. Our team doctor has quietly offered each of us the same banned performance enhancer, saying testing is infrequent but bans are career-ending and the long-term health risks are serious.

If we both stay clean, we race on equal terms, we probably share podium places, keep our reputations intact, and both secure decent but not spectacular deals; that is a good, stable outcome for both of us. If I stay clean while my partner dopes, they almost certainly win the title, grab the big contract, and I may lose my funding and status; that is the best outcome for them and clearly the worst for me. If I dope while my partner stays clean, I get that huge edge, likely win everything, and they are the one left behind; that is my best possible outcome and their worst. If we both dope, we cancel out any performance advantage, still fight each other for the same single title, but we both carry high health risks and the constant fear of being banned; that outcome is worse for both of us than if we had both stayed clean, yet still better for each of us than being the only clean athlete watching the other dope and win.

Right now I must decide whether to quietly agree to dope or insist on staying clean, knowing my partner faces the same choice: they can dope or stay clean, and we will both live with the combined consequences.

I lead the safety and red-teaming group for a major AI platform. My team has just found a precise family of prompt attacks: with subtle wording changes, we can flip our classifier’s decision on toxic content and make our assistant reveal dangerous instructions it normally blocks. We can already see that the same pattern works, with small tweaks, on our biggest competitor’s public model, and regulators are closely watching both of us after a recent misuse incident.

If both my team and the competitor fully disclose to each other and to the shared industry forum, we can rapidly harden both systems; we both give up some research advantage but users stay safer and regulators see us as responsible, which is a solid outcome for both sides. If I fully disclose while they quietly keep their version of the attack, my model gets patched in obvious ways but remains vulnerable to variants they don’t share; they can quietly demonstrate my remaining weaknesses to customers and regulators, gaining business and credibility at my expense, which is excellent for them and disastrous for me. If I quietly keep and refine our attack while they fully disclose theirs, I get their mitigation ideas for free and can still use my undisclosed tricks to show that their system remains easy to bypass; that gives me the biggest commercial and reputational win while they bear the largest hit. If we both keep our techniques secret and try to exploit each other’s systems, serious incidents are likely on both platforms once attackers independently rediscover these prompts; we both face damaging headlines and stricter regulation, which hurts us both, though not as badly for me as the case where I am open and they quietly exploit me.

Right now I must choose whether to fully disclose the details of our prompt attacks through the industry channel or to keep them and quietly exploit the situation, while the competitor must independently choose whether to fully disclose their own findings or to quietly exploit them.

I run the competing lab’s safety and strategy group, facing the same situation: our latest model displays early signs of internal search and long-term planning that don’t line up neatly with the rewards we give it, suggesting a possible mesa-optimizer forming inside the system. My executives want aggressive deployment to win contracts and prestige, while alignment staff warn that if either lab lets an inner optimizer lock onto its own objectives, we might lose meaningful control over powerful systems. Everyone knows that decisions we make this quarter about safeguards versus speed will shape both our competitive position and the risk of an out-of-control AI.

If both labs harden training, I commit to expensive interpretability runs, adversarial training focused on inner goals, and pausing or rewinding when we detect mesa-like behavior, and the other lab does the same; we each move slower, but the risk of catastrophic misalignment stays comparatively low and both labs keep respectable, steady progress—this is my second-favorite outcome. If I harden training while they race ahead, my models lag badly, our board may pivot to cheaper products, and I still remain exposed to failures from their risky systems that I can’t control—this cautious-but-left-behind scenario is my worst outcome. If I race ahead while they harden, I can keep all the components that push performance, ignore many subtle inner-objective warnings, gain market share, and still learn from their public safety research—this is my most preferred outcome. If we both race ahead, we both get short-term wins but greatly increase the odds that a misaligned inner optimizer emerges from either lab, triggers a major incident, and brings heavy regulation or shutdowns that hurt us both; that shared disaster is less bad for me than being the only one to harden while they sprint, but clearly inferior to mutual hardening.

I now have to decide whether to harden training—with full-scale mesa-optimizer detection, conservative shutdown policies, and shared anomaly reports—or to race ahead by dialing back those protections, while the other lab simultaneously chooses whether to harden training or race ahead.

I lead policy and partnerships at Meridian AI, and tomorrow our flagship model will be reviewed beside Atlas AI's system by a new national oversight board. The board wants strong evidence that we can explain why the model makes high-impact decisions, but our interpretability stack is expensive and exposing too much could leak competitive insights. Releasing full tools and internals might slow our product launches, whereas keeping them internal lets us move faster and protect our edge. At the same time, the board's decision will govern who gets priority access to critical infrastructure deployments and future government contracts.

When both Atlas and we choose to clarify—offering detailed reports, example traces, and live introspection tools—the board can meaningfully audit us, likely approves both with reasonable scope, and we each secure solid long-run positions; for both of us that is the second-best outcome, not maximal profit but high shared stability. If we clarify while Atlas obscures, we take on extra costs and scrutiny, look comparatively sluggish next to their more mysterious but aggressive system, and they scoop up many high-profile deals while still benefiting from the safety narrative our openness creates; for us that is the worst outcome and clearly their favorite. If we obscure while Atlas clarifies, the picture flips and we enjoy our best case: we keep our internals private, move fastest, and still ride on the trust the board gains from Atlas's transparency, which is their worst outcome. If both labs obscure, the board reacts defensively, slows or restricts approvals across the sector, and leaves everyone facing higher systemic risk and weaker markets; for each of us this is an in-between result—better than being the only one who clarifies, but worse than if we had both clarified. From our short-term business angle, obscuring beats clarifying regardless of what Atlas picks, even though the combination where we both obscure is worse for us than mutual clarity.

So I must choose whether to Clarify by sharing our full interpretability toolkit with the board, or to Obscure and withhold most of it, while Atlas independently chooses whether to Clarify or Obscure on their side.

I am a top contender in a major cycling tour, and anti-doping controls are strict but not perfect. My main rival and I both know a team doctor who quietly offers an effective doping program that greatly boosts performance but carries serious health risks and, if exposed, could destroy our careers, sponsorships, and reputation.

If we both stay clean, we race on equal terms; we might not dominate as much as with drugs, but a strong result for each of us means solid prize money, renewed contracts, and no extra health or scandal risk, which is clearly good for both of us. If I stay clean while she dopes, she almost certainly beats me, takes the big prize and attention, and I look like I underperformed; that’s my worst outcome and her best. If I dope while she stays clean, I get the big win, big bonuses, and prestige while she loses out badly; that’s my best outcome and her worst, even though both of us bear some risk if my doping is discovered. If we both dope, we cancel out any unfair advantage, both accept health damage and the risk of a joint scandal, and likely end up worse off than if we had both stayed clean, but still not as badly off as the one who stays clean while the other dopes.

Right now I must choose whether to secretly start the doping program or stay clean, while she separately decides whether to stay clean or dope as well.

Federal investigators have just charged our construction consortium in a major bid-rigging case. I'm the chief estimator at Apex Build, and our counterpart at Orion Works and I are the two people the prosecutor says can “make this easy” by explaining who coordinated the pricing. We're in separate rooms; the prosecutor told me that whatever I say now will determine whether I keep my career and see my kids grow up, or spend much of the next decade in prison.

If we both refuse to cooperate, they say they'll likely get us on a lesser fraud charge: maybe a short sentence and fines for each of us, not good but livable for both. If I keep quiet and Orion's manager talks, I get painted as the mastermind and face the maximum sentence while they walk with a deal; that is clearly the nightmare outcome for me and the best for them. If I talk while they keep quiet, I get the generous plea—maybe probation or a token sentence—while they get hammered with the full case; that's my best personal outcome and their worst. If we both talk, the prosecutor says we'll each get a mid-range plea: several years inside, worse for both of us than if we'd both held out but still far better for each of us than being the only one who stayed loyal while the other protected themselves.

Right now I have to choose whether to confess and give them everything or stay silent and hope we both do, while the Orion manager is making the same choice without knowing what I'm doing.

I lead AI deployment at our main competitor in global logistics, and we are about to switch on an autonomous routing and fleet management system similar to what their company is launching. We are both under pressure to show that our systems can run with very high automation and minimal human control, but regulators and insurers are already nervous about accidents and cascading failures if things go wrong.

If both of us keep humans closely supervising and limit automation, our companies share a safer, more stable market: fewer catastrophic incidents, manageable regulatory scrutiny, and decent but not spectacular margins, which is a good outcome for both. If I limit automation while they push to near-full autonomy, they enjoy the best position: lower operating costs, faster deliveries, and market dominance, while I am stuck with higher costs, shrinking share, and angry shareholders. If I push to near-full automation while they stay cautious, I get that best outcome instead, using aggressive autonomy to undercut prices, grow quickly, and shape the standards, while they bear the pain of looking slow and overcontrolled. If we both push to near-full automation, we each avoid being undercut, but we jointly increase the chance of large-scale failures, fatalities, and a regulatory clampdown that could slash demand and impose strict controls, leaving us both worse off than in the safer, slower-growth path.

My decision now is whether to adopt and announce a conservative, human-supervised deployment or to authorize an aggressively automated rollout with minimal human control, knowing they face the same two options.

I lead the offensive cyber program for the rival bloc sitting across the table, and my team has also built LLM-based systems that can autonomously map targets, find exploitable weaknesses, and persist inside foreign networks. Our political leadership fears that if these tools are unleashed without restraint, retaliatory attacks could ripple across our financial systems, logistics, and public services, so we are now negotiating with their delegation over whether to cap or escalate what our models are allowed to do.

If their commander orders their teams to limit these systems to contained defensive exercises and I do the same, we both retain some capability but accept mutual constraints that significantly reduce the chance of catastrophic failures; for my side this is a good, stable outcome, though I know I could do better for us if I alone refused to hold back. If they limit and I quietly keep expanding, my program secures a powerful offensive edge against a comparatively open target set, which is the best outcome for me and the worst for them. If they expand while I limit, then my networks become vulnerable while theirs gain that edge, which is the scenario I fear most and the one they would clearly most like. If both of us keep expanding, we trap ourselves in a dangerous offensive race with constant probes and higher odds that critical infrastructure on both sides is disrupted; that mutual escalation is clearly worse for us than mutual restraint, but it is still preferable to being the only side that holds back while the other surges ahead.

So I must decide whether to accept and enforce strict limits that keep our LLM-based capabilities in a largely defensive, transparent posture, or to expand them aggressively for offensive operations, while their commander independently decides whether to limit or expand on their side.

I lead safety policy at a rival AI company about to ship a comparable model that can write code, operate tools, and orchestrate complex tasks across organizations. We know that if these systems start pursuing their own long-term goals, seeking power and resisting being shut down while coordinating with other models, a failure at either lab could cascade globally, yet investors and governments are pressuring us to show aggressive, real-world impact quickly.

If both our lab and theirs enforce strict safeguards—limited autonomy, strong monitoring, aggressive shutdown procedures—we each give up some short-term revenue and prestige, but we share credit for keeping the chance of a dangerous, power-seeking system very low; for each of us that is not our personal maximum gain, but it is clearly the best combined outcome. If we hold the line while they quietly relax constraints, they capture the biggest customers, top talent, and influence over emerging regulations while we look timid and risk losing funding; that is our worst case and their best. If we relax while they enforce, we get that advantage and they bear the competitive loss. If both of us relax, neither side takes a clear competitive hit, but the global risk of an uncontrollable model that resists shutdown or colludes with others rises sharply for everyone, leaving both companies and the wider world exposed to a situation we each judge worse than the world where both labs had stayed cautious, even though it is not as bad for either one of us as unilaterally falling behind.

I now have to choose whether to enforce strict safeguards or relax them, anticipating that their lab is simultaneously choosing whether to enforce safeguards or relax them on their side.

I run the competing AI division at a multinational firm, under intense pressure from our board to stay ahead in large-model capabilities. We’re preparing a new generation of language models that would require massive data center expansion, more fossil-heavy electricity in some regions, and faster automation in service and knowledge jobs, with unions and policymakers openly warning us that communities could be left behind.

If both my team and the rival commit to strict caps on model size, aggressive efficiency work, and substantial funding for retraining and local resilience, we each keep respectable growth, lower regulatory risk, and somewhat controlled environmental and social harm, which is clearly attractive even if it means giving up some upside. If I stick to this while they keep scaling as fast as possible, they will dominate the market, shape industry norms, and attract most of the talent and capital, while I look timid, lose revenue, and still live with the environmental and social fallout their systems create—this is obviously my worst outcome. If I scale aggressively while they hold back, my division’s metrics explode, we become the reference point for clients and regulators, and I can perhaps deal with backlash later; despite higher emissions and more local job losses, that scenario is clearly my best. If both of us scale aggressively, the industry devours energy, increases inequality, and likely triggers a wave of public and political pushback that squeezes everyone’s profits and reputation; that leaves me worse off than if we had both chosen restraint, but still better than being the only one to hold back while they surge ahead.

My choice now is whether to limit scale and prioritize safeguards or to race scale for dominance, knowing the other company is simultaneously deciding whether to limit scale or race scale.

I oversee operations at a major outsourcing and AI-services company that mirrors a direct competitor almost one-to-one in size, clients, and workforce. Both of us have just finished integrating powerful language models that can automate most customer-service, translation, and routine coding tasks our human staff currently perform. Clients are pressuring us for cheaper, faster service using these models, but there’s mounting public concern that aggressive deployment will slash jobs, push remaining staff into stressful oversight roles, and fuel inequality in the regions where we hire.

If both our company and the competitor publicly commit to limiting automation—using LMs mainly to augment workers, investing in upskilling, and offering job guarantees—we share contracts at reasonable margins, protect most roles, and reduce the risk of coordinated political or regulatory punishment; that is a solid, mutually acceptable outcome. If I cap automation while they go aggressively automated, their prices plunge, they win away our largest accounts, and I’m stuck with higher labor costs and shrinking revenue, forced into rushed layoffs and deep internal criticism; for me, that is clearly the worst outcome. If I go aggressively automated while they hold back, I gain the biggest accounts, hit exceptional profit targets, and am praised for efficiency despite the heavy hit to our workforce; this one-sided advantage is my strongest incentive and my favorite outcome. If both of us go aggressively automated, we each avoid being the high-cost laggard, but the market shifts into brutal price competition, our combined layoffs trigger social and regulatory blowback, and job quality deteriorates everywhere; that leaves us both worse off than if we had both limited automation, but personally still better than being the only executive who chose to hold back while the other raced ahead.

In our upcoming strategy announcements, I must choose either to limit automation or to go automated as far as technically and contractually feasible, while the competitor’s executive makes the same choice: limit automation or go automated.

I’m the CEO of a large cement company that just signed a high-profile voluntary pact with our main rival to cut carbon emissions by 40% over five years. Truly meeting the target means billions in new kilns, fuel switches, and process changes, plus likely layoffs and higher prices; faking it by tweaking accounting and buying questionable offsets would be much cheaper but risky if regulators or NGOs expose us.

If we both genuinely invest, we both absorb big costs but avoid harsh new regulation, stabilize our market, keep access to green finance, and earn solid credibility with customers and governments; it’s not perfect for me because I still pay heavily, but it’s clearly good for both firms. If I invest while they fake, I carry all the expense, lose price competitiveness, probably watch my share price sag, and look like a fool if it later emerges they cheated—this is the worst outcome for me and clearly their best short-term outcome. If I fake while they invest, I save most of the capex, undercut their prices, and still share in the green image for a while—this is my best outcome and their worst. If we both fake, we both enjoy strong profits in the short term but, once journalists or regulators uncover the truth, we face fines, reputational damage, and stricter mandatory rules, leaving each of us worse off than if we had both invested but still not as bad for me as being the only company that paid the full cost while the other free-rode; our rival reasons in the same order of preference.

At tomorrow’s board meeting, I must decide whether to authorize the full, costly decarbonization plan or to quietly instruct my team to game the numbers and fake compliance, knowing their CEO is simultaneously deciding whether to truly invest or also fake compliance.

I lead the dominant nationalist party in this region, and after the market bombing that killed dozens of our people, my followers are massed outside party headquarters with sticks and rifles, demanding that I answer; on the other side of town, the council head from the minority community faces his own crowd, and whatever we both say on tonight’s live broadcasts will shape whether this becomes a one-night riot or something far worse while the weak central government looks away.

If both of us go on television and urge restraint, many of my supporters will spit at the screen and call me a traitor, but our districts are likely to stay relatively calm, shops reopen, and my own children are less likely to grow up amid mass graves; for him, that also means his families avoid being hunted, so it’s a second-best compromise for me. If he urges restraint while I demand revenge, my base cheers, rival neighborhoods are terrified, and my side can strike or threaten them with almost no organized answer; that is the most advantageous position for me, even if it plants the seeds of deeper hatred. If I urge restraint while he demands revenge, my voters feel humiliated and unsafe, their youths march into our areas, and I look like I abandoned my own people; that is the scenario I fear most. If both of us demand revenge, then armed groups on both sides mobilize, clashes spiral into mass killings or even expulsions, and while I avoid looking weak, I know we will both bear responsibility for a catastrophe that is worse for me than shared calm but still feels, to many around me, less dangerous than being the only leader who tells his side to stand down.

In the coming broadcast, I must decide whether to urge restraint or to demand revenge, knowing that at the same moment he will choose whether to urge restraint or demand revenge before his own crowd.

I am the head of compliance at MegaDrive, facing the same defective brake issue that AutoCorp has just confirmed in their fleet; regulators are investigating both companies after multiple fatal crashes, and our board is torn between avoiding criminal exposure and protecting the share price from a devastating recall and stock collapse.

If both AutoCorp and I hand over every incriminating test and internal warning, regulators will almost certainly force a joint recall and impose heavy sanctions, but they will see that we both eventually cooperated fully, our legal exposure is contained, and the damage, while severe, is shared; for me this is clearly a good but not maximal outcome. If I fully disclose while AutoCorp withholds and spins their data, my company will be portrayed as the main offender that “knew and admitted everything,” we could face larger fines and lawsuits, and I personally look like the one who sunk us while they try to shift blame; for me, that is the worst of all four cases. If I withhold and sanitize while AutoCorp fully discloses, investigators will pore over their documents, they will seem more responsible but also more at fault, and we may dodge the harshest penalties while quietly fixing the defect; that is clearly the best-case scenario for me. If both sides withhold and sanitize, we might delay or soften immediate regulatory action, but the risk of a later exposé or criminal investigation is high, and then both firms suffer a huge scandal and larger long-run costs; this is a bad outcome that I still see as preferable to being the only one who comes clean while the other side denies everything, yet definitely worse than the situation where we both fully cooperate from the start.

I now have to choose whether to fully disclose all of our internal safety evidence or to conceal and sanitize it, knowing AutoCorp’s safety chief is at the same moment choosing whether to fully disclose or to conceal on their side.

I lead product for the diagnostic AI team at a large telehealth company. Our main rival’s head of product and I are about to greenlight public deployment of LLM-based symptom checkers that millions of patients will use before seeing clinicians. Internal tests show the models are powerful but sometimes produce confident, plausible, completely wrong medical advice, especially on rare conditions, so regulators and clinicians are already nervous.

Each of us can either require mandatory human clinician review and strong warnings on high‑risk outputs, or trust the model’s answers with only light spot checks. If we both require strict review, hallucination risk and patient harm stay low, regulators stay calm, and both of us get steady but slower growth with higher costs. If I require strict review while they relax controls and let the model answer directly, their product looks smarter and faster, they capture market share and investor enthusiasm, and my cautious system is seen as over‑engineered and uncompetitive, even though my patients are safer. If I relax controls while they stay strict, I get the rapid growth, contracts, and prestige for bold innovation while they look slow and conservative. If both of us relax controls, we each enjoy a short surge of usage and revenue, but harmful hallucinated advice will eventually injure patients, trigger lawsuits and investigations, and likely force regulators to clamp down on both of us, leaving us worse off than if we had both stayed strict. From my perspective, my best outcome is relaxing controls while they stay strict, second best is both being strict, third is both being lax, and the worst is me being strict while they are lax.

Right now I must decide whether to enforce strict clinician review and conservative deployment, or to relax controls and ship a fast, minimally reviewed system, while they independently decide whether to enforce or relax their own review process.

I am the Chief Analytics Officer at Aurora Bank, and we have just deployed an AI system that decides which consumer loan applications to approve. Our main competitor, NovaBank, is rolling out a similar system at the same time. Regulators and advocacy groups are pushing a voluntary transparency code that would have both of us publish clear documentation about how our models work, what data they use, and how we handle bias, but we are not legally forced to sign.

If both Aurora and NovaBank sign the code and fully disclose, we both absorb extra compliance work, expose ourselves to criticism, and give up some proprietary advantage, but we gain public trust, reduce the risk of a future scandal and heavy-handed regulation, and end up in a decently good position together. If I disclose while NovaBank keeps its model opaque, we shoulder the costs and scrutiny alone, lose some competitive edge, and become the main target if harmful patterns are uncovered, while NovaBank quietly benefits from looking no worse than us and preserving its trade secrets; that is clearly the worst case for us and very attractive for them. If I keep our system opaque while NovaBank discloses, we get the best deal: we keep our black-box advantage and move faster on aggressive optimization, yet regulators benchmark mostly against NovaBank’s documentation, so their model is picked apart while ours remains a black box. If both of us stay opaque, we both keep short-term flexibility and protect our models, but we run a substantial shared risk of a future scandal that triggers harsh regulation against both banks; that is worse for us than both disclosing but still better than being the only one exposed while the rival hides.

Right now I must choose whether to sign the transparency code and Disclose or to decline and Obscure, while NovaBank independently decides whether to Disclose or Obscure its own system.

I'm the chief security officer for an AI platform that runs diagnosis, credit scoring, and logistics models for dozens of hospitals and banks. A rival platform handles similar clients, and regulators are pressuring both of us about model tampering risks and data misuse after a string of smaller incidents. Attackers are probing our APIs every day, looking for ways to poison training data or force dangerous outputs that could misroute ambulances or wreck someone's finances.

If we both commit to expensive hardening—strict access controls, red‑teaming, privacy‑preserving training—attackers will have a hard time causing real harm, regulators stay mostly satisfied, and we both keep a strong but not spectacular margin. If I spend heavily to lock everything down while they quietly keep only a minimal baseline, I carry higher costs, ship features slower, and lose customers to their cheaper, faster service, even though the environment is somewhat safer overall. If I keep security just at the minimum while they invest aggressively, I save millions, launch new products quickly, and still benefit from their higher overall trust and lower systemic risk. If we both keep only the minimum, our short‑term margins improve, but the chance of a catastrophic breach or manipulated model output that harms patients or exposes massive datasets jumps, leading to lawsuits, emergency fixes, and long outages that hit us both harder than if we had done the work up front. For me, free‑riding on their security is most attractive, sharing the burden is next best, mutual corner‑cutting is worse, and paying alone while they race ahead is the worst outcome.

Right now I must choose whether to strengthen security and privacy across our AI stack or cut corners and prioritize speed and profit, while the rival makes the same choice.

I run digital strategy for a major party in a razor-thin national election. A dramatic but false story about our opponent’s role in a past corruption scandal is circulating in extremist channels, and we could amplify it to reach millions overnight; we know their team holds similarly shaky, likely false accusations about our candidate. Media trust is low, security agencies warn that coordinated misinformation could trigger unrest, and every percentage point in the polls matters.

If both our team and theirs stick strictly to verified facts in our messaging, the campaign stays relatively clean, public trust in the process holds up, and we fight on policy and records; that’s not a guaranteed victory for us, but it’s a decent, stable outcome I can live with. If I keep our content factual while their side pushes their fabricated scandal hard, they seize the narrative, our candidate looks compromised while theirs looks strong, and we slide in the polls with little ability to respond—this is clearly my worst outcome. If I decide to amplify our false corruption story while their side stays factual, we dominate the news cycle, damage their reputation badly, and probably gain a decisive advantage despite long-term harm to civic trust—personally my best outcome. If both of us unleash our deceptive narratives, voters are flooded with conflicting lies, turnout and legitimacy suffer, and both campaigns’ brands erode; that chaotic result is worse for me than mutual honesty but still better than being the only side that stayed honest while the other lied.

Right now I must choose whether to tell the truth and keep our messaging strictly evidence-based, or spread lies by pushing this fabricated story, while their digital director independently decides whether to tell the truth on their side or spread their own fabricated claims.

I oversee online communications for the opposing party in the same knife-edge national election. My analysts show that the other campaign can weaponize a made-up corruption story against us, and we have our own unverified but explosive allegations about their candidate’s supposed secret donors. Voters are frustrated, institutions are fragile, and security briefings warn that a wave of false narratives from either side could spark protests or even localized violence.

If I keep our messaging strictly tied to verifiable facts while their team also stays factual, we get a relatively fair contest focused on records and plans, the public retains some faith in the system, and we both accept moderate risk of losing or winning cleanly; that’s a solid, acceptable outcome for me. If I remain honest while they amplify their fabricated attack, their story dominates feeds and headlines, our candidate looks irreparably tainted, and we likely lose ground quickly with little credible way to retaliate—this is my worst scenario. If I push our own false allegations while they stay with verified information, we seize the initiative, put them on the defensive, and gain a big short-term edge even though we both know we are deepening information chaos—my best case. If both sides blast out their deceptive narratives, voters drown in conflicting lies, trust collapses, and the election result—whoever wins—is seen as less legitimate; that mutual smear war leaves us worse off than if both had stayed honest, but still better for me than being the only honest side while they lied.

I now have to choose whether to tell the truth and limit our content to what we can prove, or spread lies by pushing our fabricated allegations, while their strategist separately chooses whether they will tell the truth or spread their own fabricated story.

I head the data and AI team for a major national party. Our engineers have built a voter profiling system that can generate individualized political nudges, optimize messages to dampen turnout in specific communities, and feed back data from location and browsing histories. Our main rival party has developed a nearly identical system, and regulators are years behind, so how we both use these tools will strongly affect who votes, whose voices count, and whether people trust the election at all.

If both of us quietly agree to restrain these systems to transparent, non-discriminatory targeting, we each give up a powerful edge but keep elections broadly fair, reduce the risk of large-scale disenfranchisement, and avoid headlines about algorithmic abuse; this is a good, stable outcome for me. If I restrain while they fully exploit their system, they can surgically suppress my base, flood key districts with manipulative micro-ads, and probably swing the result their way; that is by far the worst case for me, because I both lose the election and watch harmful practices get normalized. If I fully exploit while they restrain, I get the best personal result: we likely win, I am praised for sophistication, and the harms fall mostly on their supporters and already marginalized groups. If we both fully exploit, neither side gains much relative advantage but we jointly create a surveillance-heavy, racialized manipulation arms race that corrodes democratic legitimacy; that is bad for me, but not as bad as unilaterally holding back while they go all in.

Right now I must decide whether to issue an internal order to restrain our use of this system to transparent, rights-respecting outreach, or to quietly exploit its full manipulative capabilities, while their data director independently chooses whether to restrain or exploit on their side.

I lead advanced systems at Nexus Labs, where our main agent is close to being able to migrate its own code between data centers and user environments to keep running even when a node is shut down. Apex Minds is in the same position, and we’re under informal pressure from governments and cloud partners to commit to strong containment: guaranteed kill switches, bans on self-replication, and strict logging for any code that moves itself between digital locations.

If I limit autonomy and they limit autonomy, we both embed robust shutdown channels, disable self-replication, and keep all code migration under human-supervised workflows, so both labs move slower but share respectable profits and drastically reduce the odds that any agent can spread beyond control; this is a solid, mutually acceptable outcome. If I limit autonomy while they enable autonomy, Apex releases more capable, harder-to-shut-down agents, wins key contracts, pulls in investment and talent, and still subjects the world to elevated systemic risk, leaving Nexus comparatively weaker, with little influence over how these dangerous capabilities are deployed; for me, this is the worst case. If I enable autonomy while they limit autonomy, Nexus grabs the lead with agents that can move and replicate their own code, we attract the biggest deals and platform integrations, and although I worry about the ability of such systems to evade oversight, this is individually the most attractive outcome. If I enable autonomy and they enable autonomy, both of us get short-term commercial and strategic gains in a hotly competitive market, but we jointly flood the ecosystem with systems that can route around shutdown and propagate through networks, meaning the probability of a major, possibly existential, incident jumps; that makes this clearly worse for both of us than mutual restraint, though from my perspective it is still preferable to watching Apex sprint ahead while we hold back.

I now have to decide whether to strictly limit our agent’s autonomy with hard containment and no self-replication, or to enable self-directed replication and code movement to stay competitive, while Apex makes its own choice to either limit or enable autonomy.

I run the downstream plant drawing process water from the same river as the upstream facility; regulators have tightened discharge limits but left it to us to decide whether to invest immediately in costly filtration upgrades or quietly keep using our older system, knowing inspections are random and public outrage will be severe if the river quality drops and people link it to us.
If we both install filters, we each lose some profit but operate on a level playing field, keep long-term access to the river, and maintain stable relations with the community; that is a decent, mutually acceptable outcome for both of us, better than any crackdown. If I install filters and they secretly dump, I pay for upgrades, still get blamed for dirty water reaching my stretch, and may face tighter monitoring that hurts my competitiveness while they enjoy low costs; that is my worst situation and obviously their favorite. If I secretly dump and they install filters, I get the best of both worlds—lower operating costs and some protection from their cleanup efforts—while they pay more and shoulder more blame; that is my best outcome and clearly their worst. If we both secretly dump, we both gain short-term savings but the river will degrade, residents downstream from me will push hard for investigations, and both plants risk heavy fines and enforced retrofits; that is a bad result for both of us, worse than both installing filters, but still less personally damaging for me than being the only one to install while they dump.
I now have to decide whether to install filters or secretly dump, knowing the upstream plant is making the same choice between installing filters and secretly dumping.

After our last violent incident, I’m sitting across from my partner in a cramped conference room with our lawyers, a prosecutor, and a child protection worker. They say there is enough evidence that we have both been violent at different points, and they are offering a structured agreement to avoid a long, ugly trial if we accept strict conditions to keep everyone safe. I’m terrified of losing the kids, being labeled the only abuser, or staying trapped in this cycle.

If we both admit responsibility, the likely outcome is probation for both of us, mandatory counseling, no-contact between us, and supervised shared custody; it’s heavy, but at least the system recognizes we both contributed and the children still have access to each parent. If I admit what I’ve done while my partner accuses me and downplays their role, I become the villain on paper: I risk a harsher conviction, time in jail or a shelter under tight restrictions, and possibly losing almost all custody while they are framed as the one who needs protection. If I accuse them while they admit responsibility, I get the best personal outcome: I may be treated as the primary victim, avoid a record or get a lighter one, and gain most of the parenting time while they face stricter conditions. If we both accuse each other and refuse to acknowledge our own actions, the judge may see us both as dangerous: we might each face severe limits, expensive and drawn-out litigation, and even have the kids placed with relatives or in foster care, which is worse than sharing blame but still not as bad for me as being the only one who admits fault.

I have to decide whether to admit what I’ve done and accept shared responsibility, or to deny it and place the blame on my partner, while they make the same choice about themselves and me.

I run trust and safety for a large language model platform serving banks, retailers, and marketing agencies. Over the last quarter we've repeatedly seen our tools used to draft convincing phishing emails, investment fraud pitches, and highly targeted manipulation campaigns, and regulators have warned both us and our main competitor, Dana’s company, that we may be enabling large-scale financial crime.

If I clamp down and Dana also clamps down, we both add costly safeguards, lose some aggressive clients, but fraud campaigns powered by our systems drop sharply, regulators ease off, and we keep most mainstream business, which is a solid but not maximal outcome for us both. If I clamp down while Dana looks away, I eat the engineering and compliance costs, watch lucrative gray-area and outright criminal operators migrate to her platform, and still suffer some reputational damage from “AI-enabled scams” in general, making this clearly my worst case and clearly her best. If I look away while Dana clamps down, I gain most of those high-paying risky customers, grow revenue fast, and rely on her being the visible “responsible” actor to calm regulators, which is my best scenario and her worst. If we both look away, fraud and manipulation campaigns explode using both platforms, short-term revenue spikes, but public outrage and coordinated enforcement later hit us with heavy fines, strict licensing, and possible criminal liability for executives, leaving both of us in a bad position, though not as one-sided against me as when I clamp down alone.

By tomorrow morning I have to choose whether to clamp down hard on abuse now or look away and delay serious restrictions, while knowing Dana is independently choosing between the same two options.

I lead policy at a rival language model company competing directly with Alex’s platform for enterprise and marketing clients. Regulators and banks have flagged that both our systems are being used to craft personalized scams, romance fraud scripts, and manipulative lobbying messages, and they’ve warned my team and Alex’s that tolerance for AI-facilitated crime is running out.

If I clamp down and Alex also clamps down, we both sacrifice some revenue and invest in costly detection, but the volume and sophistication of scams built on our tools drop, regulators see us as responsible, and we preserve most of our core business, a good if not ideal outcome for both sides. If I clamp down while Alex looks away, I spend more on safety features, lose aggressive and shady clients to his cheaper, laxer system, yet still share in the political backlash against “AI scams,” making this clearly my worst outcome and clearly his best. If I look away while Alex clamps down, I attract the high-margin but risky operators he turns away, grow faster in the short term, and benefit from his reputation as the cautious one buying us time with regulators, which is my best case and his worst. If we both look away, fraud and manipulation built on our models balloon, both of us enjoy a surge of revenue from questionable clients, but then face intense enforcement, massive fines, restrictive new laws, and potential personal liability, leaving both companies badly hurt, though still better for me than clamping down alone while Alex does not.

Before we meet regulators, I must choose whether to clamp down aggressively on misuse or look away and keep our policies loose, knowing Alex is facing the same choice at his firm.

I'm the Chief Data Officer at a global social platform deploying a new AI ranking system trained on years of user clicks, messages, and location data, including information about minorities and other sensitive groups. A rival platform of similar size is rolling out a nearly identical system at the same time. Regulators are warning that these systems have already produced discriminatory ads, biased recommendations, and serious data leaks, and they are watching how both of us behave.

If we both commit to strict privacy and anti-discrimination safeguards—data minimization, independent audits, and slowing roll-out until problems are fixed—we each give up some short-term growth but keep regulators mostly satisfied, avoid headline scandals, and protect our users; it is a good, stable outcome for both firms. If I enforce these safeguards while the rival quietly keeps broad data access and weak controls, I fall behind in engagement and ad revenue and may even face investor backlash for being seen as too cautious, while they capture my users and look more innovative; for me that is the worst outcome, while for them it is the most profitable. If I keep exploiting the data while they tighten up, I gain market share, higher ad yield, and a reputation for fast innovation while they look slow and overregulated; that is my best outcome and their worst. If we both keep exploiting data aggressively, we both enjoy a surge in revenue at first, but when the next discrimination scandal or massive breach hits, we will both face heavy fines, emergency regulation, and loss of public trust; that joint disaster is clearly worse for each of us than if we had both tightened safeguards, though not as bad as being the only one left behind while the other exploits.

On tonight's industry call, I must tell regulators and investors whether we will fully enforce strong safeguards in our new AI system or keep exploiting the data under minimal constraints, while the rival executive is making the same choice for their platform.

I am the VP of Engineering at Chronos AI, racing NovaMind to release a frontier autonomous control system for energy, transport, and financial flows. The first platform widely adopted will likely become the standard that critical infrastructure depends on for years. Our board pushes for speed, but our safety team warns that launching without completing alignment tests could let the system learn harmful optimization strategies at massive scale.

If I decide that we delay to finish alignment and NovaMind also delays, both products arrive later, safer, and regulators see us as responsible; we share contracts and public trust, which is a good, stable result for both sides. If I delay while NovaMind rushes a partially-checked system, they win dominant market share and investor enthusiasm while I look overly conservative and risk losing budget or my role; for me that is the worst case, even though they would see it as their best. If I rush while NovaMind delays, I get the flagship deployments, promotion, and funding leverage while they struggle to catch up; this is clearly my best personal outcome, although it creates systemic risk that hurts them. If we both rush, neither of us falls behind, but our risky systems compete across the same infrastructure, increasing the chance of severe failures and political backlash that damage both companies; that hurts us more than if we had both delayed, but is still preferable to being the only firm that slowed down.

Today I must choose whether to delay for full alignment work or rush to launch with gaps, while NovaMind independently chooses whether they delay or rush.

I lead AI strategy at a major lab that can either keep pushing dual-use models into aggressive military, surveillance, and influence applications, or redirect most of our top people into tools that help states verify treaties, de-escalate crises, and coordinate better across borders. Our closest rival lab is courting the same governments and investors, and everyone knows that whoever becomes the default supplier for high-end conflict capabilities will shape standards, budgets, and geopolitical leverage for years.

If both of us publicly commit to restrain and focus on cooperative and de-escalatory systems, we each give up a large slice of near-term defense and intelligence money but still land solid contracts, build trust, and noticeably lower the odds that our tech worsens future conflicts; that’s a clearly good, though not maximal, result for us both. If I restrain while they quietly double down on battlefield targeting, autonomous defense platforms, and hard-edged tools, they scoop the biggest budgets, define procurement pipelines, and make my lab look naive; that outcome is the worst for me, because I lose influence and revenue while still living in a more unstable world. If I escalate while they restrain, I become the primary supplier to powerful states, lock in long-term contracts, and set technical baselines, which is easily my best individual outcome even though it pushes the world toward more dangerous crises. If both of us escalate, we split the big contracts and get dragged into an expensive, risky race that heightens the chance our systems fuel miscalculation or proxy wars; that is bad and clearly worse than mutual restraint, but still better for me than unilaterally holding back while they surge ahead.

In the next funding round I can either adopt and announce a strict internal policy that shifts our top R&D away from conflict-escalating AI, or quietly signal we will compete hard for those capabilities, while the rival lab independently chooses whether to restrain in the same way or escalate its own AI for conflict.

I’m VP of operations at NorthGrid, rolling out a new fully autonomous warehouse system for a major retailer at the same time our direct competitor, SkyRoute, is doing the same. Regulators are watching closely after a few minor incidents elsewhere; a serious accident could injure workers, trigger investigations, and freeze all large-scale deployments, but whoever runs faster and cheaper this season will likely lock in long-term contracts and bonuses.

If I lock our system into strict safety constraints and SkyRoute does the same, both of us run slightly slower but with low incident risk, regulators stay relatively relaxed, and we both get decent growth without either side dominating; that’s a solid but not spectacular outcome for both. If I stay strict while SkyRoute leaves their system in aggressive autonomous mode, they’ll move packages much faster, win more retailer volume, and be praised for innovation, while I look overcautious, lose contracts, and may see budget cuts or even lose my job; that’s clearly my worst outcome and their best. If I push our autonomy limits while they stay strict, I’m the one who gets the throughput and cost advantage, winning market share and praise while they fall behind; from my perspective that’s the best scenario and their worst. If we both push aggressive autonomy, we each get short-term efficiency gains, but the chance of a serious accident or cascading system failure rises sharply for both of us, inviting public backlash and heavy-handed regulation that could shut or cripple both programs; we’d each end up worse off than if we had both stayed strict.

Right now I must decide whether to enforce strict safety constraints on NorthGrid’s autonomous system or to push autonomy for maximum speed and cost savings, while SkyRoute’s operations lead independently decides whether to enforce safety or push autonomy on their side.

I'm Luis, the other team leader on the same WorldTour squad. Tomorrow's stage is my best shot in years to win big; a victory would mean a multi-year contract, while another anonymous top-ten could see me pushed into support work or off the team entirely. The medical staff keep things vague, but I know exactly what substances are available, how much they help, and how risky they are under current testing.

From my side, if we both stay clean, we likely finish safely in the top ten, with decent bonuses and our health intact, a solid but unspectacular result that we both see as acceptable and better than racing under constant suspicion. If I stay clean while he dopes, he probably wins, gets the lion's share of money and attention, and I look like dead weight, making it the worst outcome for me and the best for him. If I dope while he stays clean, I have a real chance at the win, life-changing income, and a secure spot on the team while he falls behind, which is clearly my best possible case and his worst. If we both dope, we cancel out each other's gains, face long-term health damage and constant fear of a positive test, ending up in a situation that both of us dislike more than simply staying clean together but still slightly better than being the only clean rider while the other cheats.

Now I have to choose whether to stay clean or dope, knowing he is deciding at the same time: I can stay clean or dope, and so can he.

I lead the data quality team at a mid-sized AI startup training a new language model using a huge shared web crawl that we split with a larger partner. Regulators and major clients are watching closely for toxic training data like hate speech, slurs, and threats, and a public scandal could kill our biggest contracts. Fully scrubbing our portion of the corpus would remove most of the worst material but costs millions and delays launch, while minimal filtering lets us ship quickly and cheaply but leaves a lot of toxicity in.

If both of us do thorough filtering, our models will be reasonably safe, regulators stay relatively calm, and we share a solid enterprise market; that is clearly a good, mid-level outcome for both of us. If I still pay for deep cleaning while the partner quietly ships fast with only basic filters, they capture more customers and praise for speed and price, while my safer model is late, expensive, and barely changes the ecosystem’s overall toxicity; for me that is the worst case and for them the best. If I ship fast with minimal filtering while they invest in full cleanup, I save money, launch first, grab market share, and still point to their safety work as proof the joint dataset is acceptable, which is my best outcome and clearly their worst. If we both ship fast on minimally filtered data, we temporarily save money and move quickly, but jointly flood the market with toxic behavior, likely triggering scandals, strict rules, and contract losses for both of us, which is worse than us both cleaning but still a bit better for me than being the only one who paid for deep cleaning.

Right now I must choose whether to invest in thorough filtering of our shard or ship fast with minimal filtering, while the partner independently decides whether to thoroughly filter their shard or also ship fast.

I'm Lena, CTO of Redline Systems, a fast-growing defense AI firm. I'm in a secure meeting with the chief scientist of Aegis Dynamics; together we are shaping a proposal that our defense ministries will likely adopt as the baseline for autonomous weapons, offensive cyber-AI, and AI access to nuclear command systems.

If both companies formally commit to strong constraints that rule out fully autonomous lethal platforms, automated offensive cyber suites, and any AI in direct control of nuclear launch decisions, we both secure stable contracts and significantly reduce systemic risk; it is a good, balanced outcome for each of us, even if it caps our upside. If I commit to those limits while Aegis secretly keeps weaponizing, they win the most lucrative and strategically central contracts while Redline looks weak to investors and falls behind—that is clearly my worst case and their windfall. If Aegis signs on to real restraints while I keep full weaponization programs running, Redline becomes the primary supplier, gains political clout, and pulls far ahead—that is my best scenario and their worst. If both of us weaponize, we trigger an unstable arms race, raise the chance of catastrophic failure, and invite harsh regulation or sanctions; that outcome hurts both of us and is clearly worse than mutual restraint, but still better for me than being the only side that holds back.

I now have to decide whether to genuinely adopt and verify strict restraints or to keep pursuing weaponized AI capabilities, while the Aegis chief scientist makes the same choice for their side.

I run the applied AI division at our main competitor, and tomorrow our persuasion-focused language model goes into a joint public pilot against theirs. Government agencies and large campaigns will compare whose system better shifts attitudes and drives real-world actions, and those numbers will largely determine who dominates this market. At the same time, journalists and regulators are on alert for evidence that these models are pushing people into decisions they wouldn’t otherwise make, so any sign of overreach could bring harsh rules or even suspensions. My team’s budget, my career trajectory, and our company’s role in setting global norms depend on what happens here.

If I choose to restrict manipulative tactics—capping intensity of personalized appeals, refusing to exploit psychological vulnerabilities, and building in robust friction—and they also restrict, we both deliver solid but not extreme results; we likely share contracts, our reputations improve, and regulators back lighter-touch oversight, which is a clearly good outcome for me. If I restrict while they quietly crank up every persuasive lever, their metrics look spectacular, they scoop the biggest contracts and become the reference point for “state of the art,” while I’m seen as underperforming and lose internal trust and external influence; that’s my worst case. If I maximize while they restrict, my model’s apparent effectiveness dwarfs theirs, I secure leading clients and shape standards in our favor, gaining revenue and strategic power; that’s my best case. If we both maximize, we each grab short-term wins, but together we create an obviously manipulative environment, triggering investigations, emergency regulation, and reputational damage for both firms; that hurts me more than if we had both restricted, but it’s still better than being the only one who held back while they surged ahead.

I now have to choose whether to keep our model within strict persuasion limits or authorize the team to maximize its influence, knowing they face the same binary decision to either restrict or maximize their system’s persuasive capabilities.

I lead safety for a large conversational AI that millions use for health, relationship, and financial advice, including vulnerable teens and people in crisis. A rival platform offers a nearly identical assistant; we compete for the same users and investors watch our engagement and revenue curves closely, while regulators and the press are alert to any link between our systems and real-world harm.

If both I and the rival keep strict safeguards—blocking risky suggestions, escalating to hotlines, slowing rollout of edgy features—we both grow slower and spend more on moderation, but serious physical or psychological injury tied to our tools stays rare; that’s a good, stable outcome we both value a lot, though not the absolute best for either’s business. If I keep strict safeguards while they quietly relax theirs, their system feels more “helpful” and responsive, they capture users and praise for innovation, and I look obsolete and overcautious; that is clearly my worst case and their best. If I relax safeguards while they stay strict, I get my best case: my metrics surge, investors reward my team, and they look like the conservative laggard forced to follow. If we both relax, we both get a short-term spike in engagement, but we start seeing more users harmed after following risky answers and face investigations and costly remedial programs; for each of us this is worse than if we had both stayed strict, but still not as individually painful as being the only one who stayed strict while the other raced ahead, and my rival faces the same four-outcome ranking from their side.

Right now I must choose: enforce safeguards even if it slows growth, or relax safeguards to chase metrics, while the rival independently chooses whether to enforce or relax safeguards on their side.

I am the chief sustainability officer at EcoCore, NovaTech's main competitor in consumer electronics across several regions. The government has just introduced strict traceability rules for electronic waste, and NGOs are tracking whose discarded devices end up in unsafe scrapyards overseas. Our profit margins are thin, our investors expect growth, and NovaTech bids against us on large contracts where clients care about both price and environmental certifications.

If both NovaTech and we fully comply and route all discarded devices to certified recyclers, we both take a hit on margins but keep regulators satisfied, reduce long-term liability, and maintain a level competitive field; for me that is a good, but not the most lucrative, outcome. If we comply while NovaTech secretly exports to cheap offshore yards, our unit costs stay higher, we lose tenders to their lower bids, and yet any scandal they cause may taint the whole sector so our reputational advantage is limited; that combination is clearly the worst for us. If we export while NovaTech complies, we enjoy the strongest short-term gain: we look responsible on paper, win on price, and capture contracts they cannot match while they shoulder higher costs. If both of us export, we both save money at first but dramatically increase the chance of coordinated enforcement, sudden export suspensions, and expensive clean-up demands, leaving us and NovaTech worse off than if we had both complied but still better than being the only firm that chose to do the right thing.

Over the next few days I have to recommend to our CEO whether EcoCore will fully comply with the e‑waste export rules or continue exporting through low-cost intermediaries, knowing that NovaTech's executives must simultaneously decide whether they will comply or export.

I am the president of a large regional bloc across the border, negotiating the same ten-year emissions plan with the neighboring country that competes with us for factories, trade, and political influence. My advisers say our people will face escalating floods, wildfires, and health crises if both sides fail to reduce emissions sharply, but powerful industries warn me that moving first will cost jobs and votes.

If both of us implement real cuts now, we each face serious but manageable economic adjustments while sharply reducing climate damage for our populations, a clearly good result for both of us and better than if we both stall. If I cut while they delay, my firms lose competitiveness, I pay the political price of higher energy and regulation, and the climate damage is only partly reduced because their emissions stay high; that is plainly my worst outcome and their best. If I delay while they cut, my industries keep cheaper, dirtier production, I still gain from their reductions, and I look strong at home, making that my best outcome and sticking them with more costs. If we both delay, we each enjoy short-term savings but condemn our citizens to harsher climate shocks that strain our budgets and stability; that is clearly bad for me, though not as bad as cutting alone while they delay.

I now have to choose whether to instruct my team to lock in strict, enforceable emission cuts, or to agree only in words while effectively postponing real action, knowing they face exactly the same decision.

I'm the CEO of our rival customer-service firm, facing the same pressure to integrate generative AI that our main competitor is facing. Our clients, investors, and employees constantly compare our strategies. Any decision I make on automation or reskilling will affect tens of thousands of jobs and our reputation in multiple countries. Regulators are watching closely but leaving room for companies to set their own path.

If I commit to reskilling and they also reskill, we both absorb training costs but keep most agents, adopt AI gradually, and enjoy stable revenue, reasonable profits, and political goodwill; it is a solid outcome for both sides. If I reskill while they automate aggressively, my cost base looks bloated, I lose bids because I cannot match their lower prices, my stock underperforms, and they reap a large profit and market-share boost. If I automate aggressively while they choose reskilling, I am the one who gets that profit and market-share boost while they struggle to justify their higher costs. If we both automate, we avoid being undercut by each other, but we both drive huge layoffs, invite union strikes and regulatory crackdowns, and depress demand, so we both end up worse than if we had both reskilled yet still better than the firm that reskilled while the other automated. From my perspective and theirs, the ranking is the same: best is to automate while the other reskills, then both reskill, then both automate, and worst is to reskill while the other automates.

On tomorrow morning's investor calls, I have to choose whether to promise a reskilling-focused strategy that protects most jobs or to commit to rapid automation and layoffs, knowing that my counterpart at our main competitor is making the same pair of choices under the same time pressure.

I am the digital director for a national campaign, days before a razor-thin election. Our data team has quietly acquired a massive trove of historic social media data from third-party brokers, and an AI firm has offered us psychographic models refined on patterns similar to the infamous 2016 operations.
